justification: Perceived representativeness is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning different political actors (parties, local/federal deputies, governor, president, municipal president), and no single actor fully captures the construct. All six items share nearly identical scales (K=5–6) and very similar entropy values (1.93–2.02 bits), making them highly compatible for averaging. A mean score reduces idiosyncratic measurement error tied to any one actor and yields a stable composite reflecting overall perceived representativeness across governance levels.

Fiscal and service federalism preferences is a broad construct spanning responsibility attribution, service management preferences, tax distribution, and power concentration, but the eight items mix distinct sub-themes (water, education, community problems, taxes, power distribution) and slightly varying scales (K=5–7), making a simple sum or mean potentially misleading. PCA on the four most conceptually central items—educational service management (p34_1), tax revenue distribution (p36), government power distribution (p38), and federal-vs-state decision concentration (p60)—extracts the dominant latent dimension of centralization vs. decentralization preference while handling scale heterogeneity. These four items have the highest entropy (1.90–2.27 bits) and best face validity for the core fiscal-governance dimension, and the first PC score is then binned into 5 quantile categories.

All six items share an identical K=5 scale measuring the same underlying preference (unified national law vs. state-specific laws) across six policy domains, making them directly combinable without rescaling. The construct is essentially unidimensional—a general disposition toward legal uniformity—and summing across domains captures the strength and breadth of that preference while averaging out domain-specific idiosyncrasies. The relatively low and uniform entropy values (1.24–1.37 bits) reflect a skewed but consistent response pattern across items, further supporting a sum score binned to ≤5 categories.

Security governance preferences spans coordination assessment, police structure preferences, state government effort evaluation, and jurisdictional assignment, but p45 (coordination assessment) and p47 (state government effort) tap evaluative/retrospective judgments rather than governance structure preferences, making them conceptually distinct from the preference-oriented items. The three preference items—police centralization vs. decentralization (p46), which level handles emergencies (p48), and which level manages public security (p49)—share compatible scales (K=6–7) and the highest entropy (1.98–2.45 bits), and PCA extracts the dominant centralization-preference dimension while accommodating minor scale differences, with the first component score binned into 5 quantiles.

Political interest and engagement is a multi-faceted construct best captured by combining news consumption frequency (p5), self-reported interest in politics (p6), perceived relevance of politics to daily life (p7), and the contrasting disposition that household well-being matters more than politics (p8, reverse-coded). All four items use scales of K=6–7 with compatible ordinal structures and entropy values ranging from 1.75 to 2.33 bits, making them suitable for averaging after reversing p8. A mean score across these complementary behavioral, attitudinal, and motivational indicators yields a more reliable and valid composite than any single item alone.
